evidence: Docstring asks for n with 'the values of φ(n+k) are all distinct', i.e. distinct consecutive values of Euler's totient function.
verify_note: decl+attr verified; totient is the statement's core object (IsDistinctTotientRun = InjOn totient); QID label exact

evidence: Docstring: Erdős, Pomerance, and Sárközy bound K when 'φ(n+k) are all distinct for 1 ≤ k ≤ K' — a statement about the totient function.
verify_note: decl+attr verified; bound on distinct-totient runs, totient load-bearing

evidence: Docstring 'the number of lines containing four points is o(n^2)' is the 4-point-line analogue of the orchard-planting problem's count of k-point lines.
verify_note: decl exists, QID live, but the orchard-planting problem (max 3-point lines) is a related problem, not a concept in this statement about 4-point lines being o(n^2); evidence itself concedes it is only an 'analogue' — tangential

evidence: Docstring: infimum of |{x : |f x| < 1}| 'over all nonconstant monic polynomials f' with real roots in [-1,1].
verify_note: decl+attr verified; statement requires f.Monic explicitly

evidence: The quantity optimized is volume {x | |f.eval x| < 1}, the Lebesgue measure of the real sublevel set.
verify_note: decl verified; the optimized quantity IS volume (Lebesgue measure on R) of the sublevel set

evidence: Docstring: supremum of |{x : |f x| < 1}| 'over all monic polynomials f' with real roots in [-1,1] is 2·2^(1/2), proved in [Tao25].
verify_note: decl+attr verified; f.Monic in the statement

evidence: Docstring: the infimum over 'all nonconstant monic polynomials f' with real roots in [-1,1] is < 1.835.
verify_note: decl+attr verified; f.Monic in the statement

evidence: Docstring: the infimum over 'all nonconstant monic polynomials f' with real roots in [-1,1] is ≥ 2^(4/3) - 1.
verify_note: decl exists with this exact (typo'd) name in 1038.lean; f.Monic in the statement

evidence: Docstring (Erdős–Herzog–Piranian Component Lemma): 'If f is a monic degree n polynomial with all roots in the unit disk'.
verify_note: decl+attr verified; h_monic hypothesis included, docstring says 'monic degree n polynomial'

evidence: The statement is about connected components of {z : |f(z)| < 1}, the interior region bounded by the polynomial lemniscate |f(z)| = 1.
verify_note: decl verified; components of {z:|f(z)|<1} are exactly the interior lemniscate regions of [EHP58], the load-bearing object

evidence: Docstring: f(z) = ∏(z - z_i) with |z_i| < 1, a monic complex polynomial with roots in the unit disk.
verify_note: decl+attr verified; f = prod(z - z_i) with h_monic included

evidence: Docstring asks for a short path inside {z ∈ ℂ : |f(z)| < 1}, the lemniscate sublevel region of the polynomial.
verify_note: decl verified; the path lives in the lemniscate sublevel region {|f|<1}, the statement's central set

evidence: Path length is defined in the file as the 1-dimensional Hausdorff measure H^1 of the path's range.
verify_note: decl verified; path length is defined in-file as muH[1] (1-dim Hausdorff measure), used directly in the statement

evidence: Docstring: 'Let f ∈ ℂ[x] be a monic polynomial' — the projection question ranges over monic polynomials.
verify_note: decl+attr verified; f.Monic hypothesis in the statement

evidence: The projected set {z : |f(z)| ≤ 1} is the filled polynomial lemniscate, per the file's levelSet definition and the [EHP58] metric-properties reference.
verify_note: decl verified; levelSet f = {|f(z)|<=1} is the filled lemniscate whose projection is the question

evidence: Docstring asks whether the projection onto a line 'has measure at most 2', formalized as volume of the projected set.
verify_note: decl verified; 'projection has measure at most 2' formalized as volume of the projected set

evidence: Docstring: Pommerenke proved that for every monic polynomial some line's projection of the level set has measure at most 3.3.
verify_note: decl+attr verified; f.Monic hypothesis in the statement

evidence: The sum ∑ 1/(t^n - 1) = ∑ τ(n)/t^n is a Lambert series; the [Er48] reference is 'On arithmetical properties of Lambert series'.
verify_note: decl+attr verified; the sum is a Lambert series (file even proves lambert_series_eq_num_divisor_sum; [Er48] title names Lambert series)

evidence: Docstring (a conjecture of Chowla): 'Is ∑ 1/(t^n - 1) ... irrational' for rational t > 1.
verify_note: decl verified; Irrational is the statement's predicate

evidence: Docstring rewrites the sum as ∑ τ(n)/t^n 'where τ(n) counts the divisors of n' — the divisor-counting function.
verify_note: decl verified; tau(n) = divisors.card is the docstring's rewrite of the sum

evidence: Docstring: Erdős [Er48] proved the sum is irrational 'if t ≥ 2 is an integer'.
verify_note: decl+attr verified; Irrational is the conclusion

evidence: The t = 2 case of this statement is the irrationality of ∑ 1/(2^n - 1), the Erdős–Borwein constant, which is what Erdős proved in [Er48].
verify_note: decl and QID both fine, but the Erdos-Borwein constant is only the t=2 instance of this all-integers-t>=2 statement; not mentioned in docstring or file — instance-of, not load-bearing

evidence: Docstring asks whether ∑ 1/(a_n·a_{n+1}) 'is irrational' for strictly increasing integer sequences with liminf a_n^(1/2^n) > 1.
verify_note: decl+attr verified; Irrational (ErdosSeries a) is the conclusion

evidence: Docstring: Erdős [Er88c] notes that if a_{n+1} ≥ C·a_n^2 'then the series is irrational'.
verify_note: decl+attr verified; Irrational is the conclusion

evidence: Docstring: 'Are there only finitely many unitary perfect numbers?' (erdosproblems.com/1052, comment 'unitary perfect numbers', OEIS A002827).
verify_note: decl+attr verified; docstring literally asks about unitary perfect numbers

evidence: The file defines unitary perfection via properUnitaryDivisors: divisors d of n with d coprime to n/d, i.e. unitary divisors.
verify_note: decl verified; IsUnitaryPerfect built on properUnitaryDivisors (d | n with d coprime n/d), the unitary-divisor definition

evidence: Docstring: 'All unitary perfect numbers are even', with an AlphaProof formal proof linked.
verify_note: decl+attr verified; 'all unitary perfect numbers are even'

evidence: Docstring asks if f(n) = o(n) 'for almost all n', formalized as a set A with A.HasDensity 1 (natural density one).
verify_note: decl+attr verified; 'for almost all n' formalized as A.HasDensity 1 — density is load-bearing

evidence: The formal statement quantifies over a set A with A.HasDensity 1 alongside the limsup f(n)/n = ∞ question.
